# AI Blog: How to Improve Professional PowerPoint Slides Generated by NotebookLM

**Source:** https://sumproduct.com/blog/ai-blog-how-to-improve-professional-powerpoint-slides-generated-by-notebooklm/

[Home](https://sumproduct.com/)

\> AI Blog: How to Improve Professional PowerPoint Slides Generated by NotebookLM

*   March 13, 2026

AI Blog: How to Improve Professional PowerPoint Slides Generated by NotebookLM
==============================================================================

_Welcome back to our AI blog series! In the previous blog, we showed how NotebookLM can turn a written paper into a structured slide deck in minutes. Using the previous article, “Audit in an AI World”, as the source, NotebookLM generated a coherent presentation that closely followed the logic, headings and messaging of the original document._

![](https://sumproduct.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/image-148.png)

This first version is impressive, but it is not yet ready to go.  
We will go one step further and look at how to improve AI-generated slides, using practical examples from our own workflow. The core lesson is simple: the quality of the output is directly linked to the quality of the sources and the precision of the prompt.

![](https://sumproduct.com/wp-content/uploads/2026/02/image-149.png)

**_Improving Slide Quality Starts with Better Sources_**  
NotebookLM does not “design” in the abstract. It learns from what we give it. Let me explain.  
In our first pass, the sources were content-focused: a Word document explaining audit risk, AI hallucination and assurance frameworks. This was enough to produce a well-structured deck, but not enough to reflect our required visual standards. To improve the visual and stylistic quality, we added another type of source, our SumProduct PowerPoint template that has been converted into PDF format.  
At the time of writing, NotebookLM does not support PPT or PPTX uploads. However, it reads PDFs very effectively. By exporting our internal slide template to PDF, including colour palette, typography, spacing and layout rules, we effectively showed NotebookLM how SumProduct slides should look.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

This simple workaround made a noticeable difference. The regenerated deck aligned far more closely with our brand style, without requiring extensive manual reformatting afterwards. Therefore, if you want AI-generated slides to match your corporate identity, upload your slide template as a PDF source before generating the deck.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

**_Using More Detailed Prompts to Guide the Output_**

The second improvement came from refining the prompt.

In the initial run, the instruction was deliberately simple: generate a slide deck based upon the uploaded sources.  For the next iteration, we added explicit guidance, including:

*   target audience (finance professionals and decision-makers)
*   slide tone (professional and instructional, not marketing-heavy)
*   preference for concise bullet points over dense paragraphs
*   alignment with SumProduct’s visual style.

NotebookLM responds well to this level of direction.  It does not simply summarise content; it reshapes it to fit the requested format.  This is particularly important when turning long-form analysis into presentation-friendly language.

****_The Reality Check: Spelling Errors and Locked PDFs_****

One limitation became clear once the deck was generated.

NotebookLM exports slides as PDF only.  This is convenient for sharing, but it also means the file is not directly editable.  In our example, we noticed several minor spelling, grammatical and wording errors.  Individually, they were small.  Collectively, they mattered.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

There are two \[2\] practical ways to deal with this.  
The first approach is preventative. Fix the problem at the source: the prompt. By explicitly instructing NotebookLM to prioritise spelling and language accuracy, the model becomes noticeably more careful. This does not guarantee perfection, but it significantly reduces errors. For professional, client-facing decks, this step is worth including every time.  
The second approach is corrective. Convert the PDF into an editable deck.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

If the slides already exist and need editing, tools such as Canva Pro or Codia AI can convert a PDF back into an editable PowerPoint file.  We tested this approach and recovered a deck that preserved most layouts and visuals, allowing us to correct text, adjust spacing and make final refinements before presentation.

One impressive outcome of this exercise was how closely the slides followed the original “_Audit in an AI World_” article.  The structure, examples and conceptual flow from AI risk and hallucination through to assurance frameworks were consistently preserved across versions.

This reinforces an important point: NotebookLM does not invent narratives.  When the source material is strong, the slides remain faithful to it.

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

**_Word to the Wise_**

NotebookLM is already capable of producing high-quality presentation drafts.  With a little extra care such as better sources, clearer prompts and a practical plan for final edits, it can become a reliable part of a professional presentation workflow.

AI will not replace judgment, proofreading or brand ownership.  However, it can remove hours of manual effort and allow us to focus on what matters most: clarity, insight and credibility.

Keep reading!  The next blog will cover audio summaries, interactive presentations, and ways NotebookLM supports various learning and communication styles. 

_Join us next time for more exciting news of AI tools!_
